coredata.dc:title: Controllable hydrogel-thermal synthesis of Mn2O3/CNT aerogels: Shape evolution, growth mechanism and electrochemical properties

Growth mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of SWCNT hydrogels with cationic surfactant
Confined growth of MnO x /CNT aerogels via hydrogel-thermal process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the hydrogel-thermal synthesis of 3D MnO x /CNT aerogels.
SEM images of MnO x /CNT aerogels, MnO x and their XRD spectrum under different precursor concentration of 0.033 M (a, d, g), 0.066 M (b, e, h) and 0.2 M (c, f, i). The scale bar is 500 nm.
Schematic illustration of the possible growth mechanism of Mn2O3 (a) nanocubes, (b) nanorods and (c) nanowires during hydrogel-thermal process.

(a) First three discharge-charge profiles of Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT nanocomposites. (b) Cyclic voltammetry curves of Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT nanocomposites at different scan rates. The inset shows the linear fitting between peak current and square root of scan rate. (c) Cycling performance of Mn2O3/CNT at a current density of 0.1 A/g. (d) Rate performance of at various current densities.

head.ce:title: Controllable hydrogel-thermal synthesis of Mn2O3/CNT aerogels: Shape evolution, growth mechanism and electrochemical properties

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of High Performance Fibers & Products, Ministry of Education, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High Performance Fibers & Products

ce:section.ce:para: Transition metal oxides (TMO) have attracted significant interest because of their physical and chemical properties and wide applications in catalysis, ion exchange, biosensors and especially energy storage such as electrodes materials in lithium ion batteries and supercapacitors [1–3]. Of various TMO, nanostructured manganese oxides MnO x (such as MnO2, MnO, Mn2O3 or Mn3O4) have been intensively studied due to their environmental friendliness, low cost and superior theoretical electrochemical properties [4,5]. However, MnO x usually has poor electronic conductivity and large volume changes during the conversion reaction, leading to the limited reversible capacity and fast capacity fade [6,7]. Forming hybrid structures with highly conductive carbons (carbon nanofibers, carbon nanotubes, graphene) is one of the most adopted methods to improve the device performance of metal oxide electrodes. A variety of techniques have been developed for the synthesis of MnO x /carbon composites. Hydrothermal method is usually used with the precursor of MnO x (KMnO4) and CNT or graphene oxide solution, and MnO x is grown on the surface of CNT [8,9], graphene [10–12] or graphene@CNTs [13]. However, these MnO x -based electrode materials usually suffer from electrochemical instabilities or low energy/power density. This maybe due to the lack of mechanical stability of electrode materials or interconnected porous network which ensures the fast electronic and ionic transport. Therefore the preparation of free-standing MnO x /C composite electrodes with porous network via facile methods is still a big challenge. Ma et al. used a ketch sponge as substrate to immerse MnO2/PEDOT/graphene for flexible supercapacitors [14]. Fan et al. [15] used butterfly wings as both 3D templates and carbon sources for 3D hierarchical MnO2/C supercapacitor electrodes. While Fang et al. used a nickel foam as substrate via layer by layer self-assembly technology for MnO2/rGO @ Ni electrodes [16]. Due to the large surface area, fast ion diffusion of 3D porous network, 3D free-standing MnO x /C electrodes usually demonstrate high electrochemical performance. However, in these methods templates or complex steps are needed which limit their applications. Zhang et al. [17] used KMnO4 and graphene oxide solution as precursor and achieved MnO2 nanorods/rGO aerogels via hydrothermal method. Sun et al. [18] obtained MnO2/rGO aerogels through immersing rGO aerogels into KMnO4 solutions. However, their electrochemical performance still needs to be improved.

Here we propose a novel method for one-step synthesis of 3D Mn2O3/CNT aerogels based on the controllable growth of nanostructured Mn2O3 in CNT hydrogel under hydrothermal conditions. This shape evolution from Mn2O3 nanocubes, nanorods to nanowires were observed. The mechanism for the formation of Mn2O3 nanostructures was discussed based on theoretical and experimental results. The electrochemical performance of 3D Mn2O3/CNT aerogels as positive materials of rechargeable Li-ion batteries have also been researched. The experimental results demonstrate that Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT aerogels provide better insertion and extraction of lithium ions, resulting from the relatively large surface area, shorter diffusion length for mass and charge transport. This “hydrogel-thermal” method is aimed to provide a universal, effective, facile way for 3D aerogel composites with controllable growth of nanostructured transition metal oxides.

Single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) were purchased from Cheap Tubes Inc. (purity higher than 90% with ashes <1.5 wt%) with the diameter of 1–2 nm and length of 5–30 μm. Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and ethanol (200 proof) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were analytical grade and used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SWCNT hydrogels with cationic surfactant
ce:section.ce:para: The cationic surfactant CTAB was firstly dissolved into deionized water and then SWCNT was added under sonication bath (12 W, Cole-Parmer 08849-00). After about 18–20 h, the whole solution turned into hydrogels. The concentration of SWCNT could range from 8 mg/mL to 60 mg/mL. In this work, the SWCNT concentration was 12 mg/mL and the weight ratio between surfactant and SWCNT was 6:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confined growth of MnO x /CNT aerogels via hydrogel-thermal process
ce:section.ce:para: In the typical synthesis procedure, a certain amount of KMnO4 was put into deionized water under stirring to get a homogenous solution. The concentration of KMnO4 was varied in the range of 0.033–0.2 mol/L. Then CNT hydrogel was immersed into this solution for about 24 h to form a gel with enriched MnO4 −. Subsequently, the total solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 24 h. When the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally, the resultant hydrogel was then washed with the mixture of deionized water and ethanol (1:1 v/v) at least three times over the course of 2–3 days. The final products were obtained after the freeze-drying for 48 h at −60 °C under 0.05 mbar pressure (FDU-1200, Shanghai Ailang Instrument Co., Ltd., China).

The bulk density of MnO x /CNT aerogels was estimated from the geometric size and weight of as-formed samples. The morphologies of pure SWCNT aerogels and SWCNT/MnO2 composites were characterized by field-emission high-resolution scanning electron microscope (JEOL 7500F SEM) at 20.0 kV. The crystalline structures of MnO2 and SWCNT/MnO2 composites were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/max 2500) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154056 nm) at V = 40 kV and I = 150 mA. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption was determined by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements using an Autosorb IQ3 (Quantachrome Instruments) surface area analyzer.

Coin-type (CR 2016, MTI corporation) half-cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box with H2O and O2 contents <1 ppm to avoid any moisture contamination. The free-standing CNT/MnO2 composites were used as the working electrode. Half cells using Li metal as both counter and reference electrodes were constructed. 1 M LiPF6 solution in EC and DEC mixed at a volume ratio of 1:1 was used as the electrolyte and Whatman GF/A (Sigma-Aldrich) was used as the separator. Galvanostatic charge and discharge measurements were carried out in the voltage range between 3.0 V and 0.01 V at different current densities using a battery test system (Arbin Instrument) at room temperature.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the alkyl ends of cationic surfactant CTAB were firstly absorbed on the surface of the single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) due to CNT's hydrophobicity. After long time sonication bath, SWCNT hydrogels were formed based on the bonding formation between carbon nanotubes through van der Waals interaction at overlapping contact points. When the CNT hydrogel was immersed into the KMnO4 solution, the negative MnO4 − would be released into the hydrogel due to the electrostatic attraction with cationic ends of CTAB, resulting in the uniform absorption of precursors within the 3D network. It is reported that KMnO4 transform into active atoms to form MnO2 nuclei due to the decomposition of KMnO4 in water, which can be formulated as:
(1)
4MnO 4
−
+
2H 2
O →
4MnO 2
+
4OH −
+
3O 2

During the hydrothermal environment of co-hydrogel, nanostructured MnO x (MnO2, Mn2O3, Mn3O4 or MnO) will be formed due to the further redox reaction with CNT according to Eq. (2) [19]:
(2)
MnO 4
−
+
H 2
O + C →
MnO x
+
OH −
+
O 2
+
CO 3
2 −
+
HCO 3
−

Accordingly, manganese oxide/CNT hydrogel could be in situ obtained. After the freeze-drying process, the 3D MnO x /CNT hybrid aerogels were formed. In such architecture, 1) the SWCNT aerogel network ensures sufficient electron transport and the homogeneous junctions serve as the transfer of electrons from CNT to the nanostructured MnO x ; 2) the interpenetrating network provides a high surface area, leading to more active sites for Li+ on the nanostructured MnO x ; 3) the interconnected pore channels facilitate access of the electrolyte to the active materials and lithium ions transport.

Fig. 2a–c show the morphologies of MnO2 nanorods, nanowires and nanoflowers under 0.033 mol/L, 0.066 mol/L and 0.2 mol/L KMnO4 as precursors at 180 °C, respectively. The X-ray diffraction patterns show that they are indexed to tetragonal β-MnO2 (JCPDS 24-0735), α-MnO2 (JCPDS 44-0141) and δ-MnO2 (JCPDS 52-0556) respectively. The shape and crystalline structure evolution were in accordance with previous report [20]. This implied that the concentration of precursor (KMnO4) plays an important role in tuning the crystalline structures and nano-morphologies of MnO2. While the reactions happen in CNT hydrogels, MnOx/CNT composites with different morphologies were shown. As indicated in Fig. 2d–f, composites with MnOx nanocubes, nanorods and nanowires were obtained. Therefore, the KMnO4 concentration during the hydrogel-thermal process also has a key effect on the shape formation. While the XRD patterns confirm that all the peaks of MnOx in MnOx/CNT composites can be indexed to a bixbyite Mn2O3 (JCPDS 41-1442). It is demonstrated that MnO4 − was transformed into Mn2O3 by further redox under the hydrogel-thermal condition with CNT:
(3)
4KMnO 4
+
H 2
O + 2C →
2Mn 2
O 3
+ KOH +
2O 2
+
K 2
CO 3
+
KHCO 3

It is worth noting that only Mn2O3 crystals were achieved even under different concentrations of KMnO4 precursor. It proves that CNTs are abundant in CNT hydrogels for the above reaction so Mn2O3 crystals would be obtained. However, there is a shape evolution from Mn2O3 nanocubes, nanorods to nanowires with the increase of KMnO4 precursor concentration. Moreover, the bulk density of Mn2O3/CNT aerogels is estimated as 0.021 g/cm3, 0.040 g/cm3 and 0.067 g/cm3, respectively. Supposed that there is no loss in weight of SWCNT during hydrogel-thermal process, the Mn2O3 fraction in three aerogels is 43%, 70% and 82%. It indicates that the growth rate of Mn2O3 is getting slow with the increase of precursor concentration. In order to characterize the pore structure, BET measurements of Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT aerogels were carried out (Fig. S2). It can be clearly seen that the isotherm shows type-IV curves, corresponding to uniform mesoporous structures. The Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT networks show a high specific surface area of 642 m2/g, with an average pore size distribution of 22.6 nm.

In addition, the manganese oxide after hydrothermal reactions or hydrogel-thermal reactions shows very different morphologies. When the concentration of KMnO4 is low (0.033 mol/L), Mn2O3 nanocubes with ~100 nm in side length were shown while nanorods were exhibited under the same concentration in hydrothermal process. When the concentration is increased to 0.066 mol/L, overlapped Mn2O3 nanorods were achieved with several micrometers in length and diameter of ~100 nm. When high concentration precursor was used after hydrothermal reaction (0.2 mol/L), MnO2 exhibits flower shape structures with diameter of 1–1.5 μm, comprising a large number of MnO2 nanowalls. However, after the hydrogel-thermal process with the same concentration of KMnO4, Mn2O3 nanowires were entangled with CNT, forming an interpenetrating structure [21].

Zheng et al. have studied the crystal formation during the hydrothermal process of KMnO4 [20] considering the effect of concentration. Different with the traditional hydrothermal environments, the hydrogel-thermal process also needs to take the effect of surfactant and hydrogel network into account. Here in order to study the role of CNT hydrogel network on the formation of nanostructured Mn2O3, we prepared Mn2O3/CNT nanocomposites using conventional hydrothermal method (see details in Supplementary information). Although the parameters including the concentrations, temperature and time are the same, Fig. S4 shows that Mn2O3 nanoparticles are coated on the surface of carbon nanotubes or aggregated into clusters, leading to a denser and nonuniform structure. It is demonstrated that the nanostructure growth of Mn2O3 is also affected by the support of 3D hydrogel network.

On the basis of our results and previous studies in hydrothermal synthesis of MnO2, the possible formation mechanism is proposed as follows and the schematic illustration of this mechanism is shown in Fig. 3 . Generally, the growth process of crystals can be separated into two steps, an initial nucleating stage and a subsequent crystal growth process. When the CNT hydrogel is immersed into the KMnO4 precursor solution, K+ and MnO4 − ions were absorbed into the hydrogel due to the electrostatic attractions with surfactants. At the first initial nucleating stage, the Mn2O3 nuclei were obtained according to the reaction process (3). According to previous studies, bixbyite Mn2O3 are all constructed from chains of [MnO6] octahydra, which are then linked in different ways to form different nanostructures [22]. When the concentration of KMnO4 precursor is very low (0.033 mol/L), the low MnO4 − ions concentration in the co-hydrogel leads to a slow decomposition rate at the initial stage. [MnO6] octahydra tends to form cubic structure, probably because this structure is the most thermodynamically stable structure of all the phases (Fig. 3a). From a thermodynamic point of view, the crystal tends to form a closed shape with low Miller index faces. With the increase of KMnO4 solution, more MnO4 − ions would be absorbed in the CNT hydrogels. In the CNT hydrogels, the organic ends were absorbed on the surface of CNT, forcing the cationic ends of CTAB exposed to the outside. So we think that lots of MnO4 − ions could be self-assembled into rods in the presence of CTAB (Fig. 3b). With the Mn2O3 nuclei formation, the [MnO6] octahydra tends to form Mn2O3 nanorods. When the KMnO4 precursor is increased to 2 mol/L, higher nuclei cause the formation of hierarchical structures at the crystal growth stage, like the formation of MnO2 nanoflowers (Fig. 2c). However, different with aqueous solutions, there is not enough space for the growth of hierarchical structures with large size in the hydrogel-thermal process. Similar to the crystal growth model in the above condition, 1D growth behavior (long nanowires) is preferential (Fig. 2g). Different from the solution environments, anomalous in situ growth of nanomaterials in hydrogel has been observed by our previous report of interpenetrating PANI nanoribbon/SWCNT composites [23]. In summary, due to the confined space in hydrogels, the nanomaterials prefer to 1D growth behavior (nanowire, nanoribbon, nanotube etc.) and the confined size of nanomaterials is good for the electrochemical performance.

Furthermore, the electrochemical properties of Mn2O3/CNT aerogels with different nanostructures were explored as anode materials for LIBs using the standard Mn2O3/CNT/Li half-cell configuration. In order to illustrate the redox reactions, the first three discharge-charge profiles and cyclic volumetric curves of Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT nanocomposites were shown in Fig. 4a and b. The first discharge and charge capacities are 1392 and 798 mA/g, respectively, with a coulombic efficiency of 57%. The high capacity loss can be explained by the irreversible formation of SEI layer and the decomposition of electrolyte at a low potential region. The coulombic efficiency of Mn2O3 nanocubes/CNT and Mn2O3 nanorods/CNT composites are in the first cycle is 53% and 46%. Two oxidation peaks at 1.4 V and 2.3 V can indicate that the electrochemical oxidation reaction may proceed by two steps. The reduction peaks at 0.47 V and 0.28 V can be assigned to the electrochemical reduction of Mn2O3 with Li. In the first cycle an extra reduction peak at 0.85 V can be devoted to the formation of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer, which is consistent with the GCD results.

Fig. 4c demonstrates the cycling performance of Mn2O3/CNT aerogels with different nanostructures at a current density of 0.2 A/g. Obviously, Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT aerogels show the highest capacity of 868 mAh/g and slow decay to 840 mAh/g after 100 cycles, indicating excellent cycling stability (97%). As a comparison, the capacity of Mn2O3 nanocubes/CNT aerogels decreases from 791 mAh/g to 722 mAh/g, only remaining 91% after 100 cycles. While the capacity of Mn2O3 nanorods/CNT aerogels decay from 830 mAh/g to 786 mAh/g, remaining 94% after 100 cycles. In order to comprehend the electrochemical behavior of Mn2O3/CNT aerogels, we also investigated its rate performance with the different current densities. Fig. 4d shows the capacities of three aerogels at various current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1 and back to 0.1 A/g. It's distinct that the capacities of three aerogels decrease with the increase of current density firstly. When the current density is decreased to 0.1 A/g, the capacity of Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT aerogels recovers to almost initial value. However, Mn2O3 nanocubes/CNT aerogels or Mn2O3 nanorods/CNT aerogels show a distinct decay in the same conditions. The energy density of Mn2O3 nanocubes/CNT, Mn2O3 nanorods/CNT and Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT composites has been calculated as 856.1 Wh/kg, 882.2 Wh/kg and 905 Wh/kg, respectively. These results suggest that Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT aerogels are more suitable for electrode materials in lithium-ion batteries. Firstly, the 3D interconnected porous tunnels provide highly effective penetration of electrolyte ions and 3D CNT network brings benefits to electron transport. Compared to Mn2O3 nanorods or nanocubes, Mn2O3 nanowires might effectively increase specific surface area and guarantee more active sites and relieve the stress during the lithium ion insertion/extraction reactions. Based on the Randles-Sevcik formula of solid state diffusion controlled process, the effective diffusion coefficient of three kinds of nanocomposites, Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT presents greater diffusion coefficient of 2.67 × 10−10 cm2/s, which also proves the advantages of Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT aerogels.

In summary, a facile hydrogel-thermal method is proposed for the synthesis of 3D Mn2O3/CNT aerogels as electrodes in lithium ion batteries. With the increase of precursor concentration, the nanostructure of Mn2O3 has a shape evolution from nanocube, nanorod to nanowire. The growth mechanism is also discussed considering the effects of concentration, surfactant and hydrogel network. The electrochemical results suggest that Mn2O3 nanowires/CNT aerogels show excellent rate capability and specific capacity of 782 mAh·g−1 at 0.2 A/g due to the effective electron/ion transport which benefits from 3D porous and CNT network and increased active surface area. This facile hydrogel-thermal strategy assisted by surfactants could be extended to other transition metal oxides for high-performance electrodes in electrochemical energy storage. Our work demonstrates a promising strategy for the design of high-performance electrode materials.

Yang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Mingxia Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Pin Zheng: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Dengteng Ge: Writing - review & editing. Lili Yang: Supervision.

Yang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Mingxia Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Pin Zheng: Writing - review & editing. Dengteng Ge: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Lili Yang: Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the financial support from the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2232017D-05), National Natural Science Foundation of China 11604045 and 11774049, the Shanghai Science and Technology Committee project (17JC400700 and 17ZR1440000), and Key Laboratory of High Performance Fibers & Products, Ministry of Education of China (2232019G-02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2019.108022.
